channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: China Seeks an Advantage With Both Trump and Iran as War Evolves
item.description: With President Trump’s visit to Beijing looming, China is pushing Iran to negotiate even as its companies export material that could be used by Iran’s military.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A government-organized march in Tehran on Wednesday in support of Iran’s new supreme leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei.

item.title: Trump Says U.S. Will Help Stranded Ships Leave Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump offered few details on how the new program, which he called Project Freedom, would work, but said interference would be dealt with “forcefully.”
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, David E. Sanger and Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, near Musandam, Oman, on Friday.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump While Stock Futures Inch Up
item.description: Oil prices fell and futures on the S&P 500 rose slightly on Sunday as investors reacted to continued uncertainty about the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Court Ruling Upends Abortion Access in the U.S., and Trump’s New Plan for the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Plus, the complicated legacy of Spirit Airlines.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Heather Knight
item.media:description: A federal appeals court temporarily halted a Food and Drug Administration regulation that has greatly expanded access to the abortion pill mifepristone.

item.title: The U.S. Army’s ‘Big Experiment’ in the Arctic Cold
item.description: How would soldiers from places like Florida, Texas and Georgia fight and persevere in temperatures as low as minus 40 degrees?
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe and Kenny Holston
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Trump Backed Forced Treatment for Homeless People. Utah Shows the Challenges.
item.description: State lawmakers declined to back a Trump-inspired plan to move 1,300 homeless people to a campus on the edge of Salt Lake City, but supporters are trying to keep the plan’s spirit alive.
item.dc:creator: Jason DeParle
item.media:description: Tyler Clancy, Utah’s new homelessness coordinator, is leading the state’s effort to design a plan to serve “high utilizers” of public services, increase shelter and housing capacity, and expand mental health care.

item.title: Bison Have Grazed These Lands for Centuries. Trump Wants to Evict Them.
item.description: The Trump administration is evicting bison herds from federal grasslands in Montana, siding with ranchers and Republican leaders over environmentalists and tribal leaders.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy

item.title: Mariachi Brothers Detained by ICE Invited to Open for Kacey Musgraves After Release
item.description: The teenagers, who had faced deportation after a high-profile detention, took the stage on Sunday thanks to an invitation from one of country music’s biggest stars.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Luis Antonio Gámez-Cuéllar wiped a tear from his face after he and his family were released from the Dilley Immigration Processing Center in Dilley, Texas, in March.

item.title: Rudy Giuliani Hospitalized in Florida in ‘Critical Condition’
item.description: A spokesman for the former mayor of New York City said Mr. Giuliani was critical but stable.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rudy Giuliani, the former mayor of New York, is critically ill in a Florida hospital.

item.title: How the Kremlin Lures Africans Into Russia’s War in Ukraine
item.description: A growing number of men across the continent say they are being promised jobs in Russia, only to be forced into the war. Some go as mercenaries, but many more are drawn unwittingly.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg, John Eligon, Zimasa Matiwane and Ed Ram
item.media:credit: Ed Ram for The New York Times

item.title: Why Are Africans Fighting in Russia’s War in Ukraine?
item.description: Some see a clear explanation: Russia needs more troops, and young men in Africa desperately need jobs.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:credit: Ed Ram for The New York Times
item.media:description: Family members of people recruited by Russia for the war in Ukraine protested in Nairobi, Kenya, in March. The lack of information on missing loved ones spurred their appeal to lawmakers.

item.title: Drone Hits a Moscow High-Rise Days Before a Major Military Parade
item.description: Air defenses in the Russian capital were breached as Ukraine expands long-range strikes.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:description: Damage to a high-rise residential building after a drone attack in Moscow on Monday.

item.title: The Met Gala’s Embrace of Jeff Bezos Causes a Backlash
item.description: Protesters have found a perfect foil in Amazon’s founder, the gala’s lead sponsor this year.
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley and Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:description: In the weeks leading up to the event, an increasingly visible anti-Bezos campaign has erupted on New York’s streets and subways. On social media, users have described the event as the “Amazon Prime Gala” or “Bezos Ball.”

item.title: The Met Makes a Statement With 9 New Mannequin Bodies
item.description: The latest Costume Institute exhibition expands its ideas of who, exactly, belongs in fashion. Will the gala follow suit?
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Paul Westlake, via The Metropolitan Museum of Art
item.media:description: Five of the new mannequins in the new Costume Institute exhibition, each of which have been modeled on actual people.

item.title: Met Gala 2026 Guide: Theme, Hosts, Dress Code and More
item.description: What to know about this year’s celebration, including the return of a superstar last seen on the Met carpet 10 years ago.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:description: Beyoncé arriving to the Met Gala in 2016, the last year she attended the event.

item.title: School Cellphone Ban Study Finds Mixed Results
item.description: Cellphone bans got devices out of students’ hands, according to the first large study. But behavior and academics have not improved, at least so far.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein
item.media:credit: Lea Suzuki/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Cellphone use declined in schools that adopted strict bans, though test scores did not immediately improve.

item.title: With World Distracted by Iran War, Israeli Settlers Intensify Attacks in West Bank
item.description: Israel has failed to arrest a wave of violence against Palestinians, prompting military officials to urge the government to intervene.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer, David M. Halbfinger and Fatima AbdulKarim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Mourners carrying the body of Amir Odeh, 28, who was killed in an attack by Israeli settlers in the village of Qusra, in the West Bank, in March.

item.title: Democrats Add 8 Candidates to House Battlefield Program for Midterms
item.description: The party’s congressional campaign arm is promoting more contenders to take down at-risk Republicans — and is taking sides in some competitive primaries.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Shane Goldmacher

item.title: Fare on PATH Trains Increases to $3.25 as Part of Revitalization Plan
item.description: Later this month, riders on the weekend will be able to travel directly from Hoboken to the World Trade Center for the first time in nearly 25 years.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.category: Port Authority of New York and New Jersey
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: PATH trains connect Northern New Jersey and Manhattan.

item.title: A Medical Examiner Chases Down an Elusive Killer
item.description: Unusual opioids are infiltrating street drugs. Knoxville’s top forensic doctor is on the front lines, pressing to quickly identify the most lethal.
item.dc:creator: Jan Hoffman and Brett Carlsen
item.media:credit: Brett Carlsen for The New York Times

item.title: What to Know About Orphines, a New Class of Deadly Opioids
item.description: The drugs are 10 times more dangerous than fentanyl. They are showing up in street drugs in the South and the Midwest, and will most likely spread to other regions.
item.dc:creator: Jan Hoffman
item.media:credit: Brett Carlsen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Knoxville, Tenn., police officer at the scene of a suspected overdose last month. The greater Knoxville area has seen a startling spike in overdose deaths driven by the presence of orphines.

item.title: Chizi, Standup Comic Exiled in China, Wants to Be More Than Just ‘a Rebel Comedian’
item.description: Having gotten into trouble for making jokes critical of the Chinese government, the standup comic Chizi now lives in self-imposed exile. He’s finding that freedom imposes its own constraints.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan and Chang W. Lee
item.media:description: Chizi performed for the first time in years in Tokyo in April. The audience went to see a rebel.

item.title: 3 Dead in Hantavirus Outbreak Aboard Cruise Ship, W.H.O. Says
item.description: One case of hantavirus infection has been confirmed in a laboratory, the organization said on Sunday. There are five additional suspected cases.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri and Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius off Cape Verde on Sunday. Officials identified the ship as the one with passengers infected by hantavirus.

item.title: What to Know About the Suspected Hantavirus Outbreak on an Atlantic Cruise Ship
item.description: Three passengers of the MV Hondius died after showing symptoms of the rare disease. Health authorities confirmed one case and are investigating five others.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius stationed off the port of Praia, Cape Verde, on Sunday.

item.title: What Is Hantavirus, Which Is Linked to the Deaths of 3 People Aboard a Cruise Ship?
item.description: Three people aboard a cruise ship sailing the Atlantic Ocean have died from what health officials suspect were hantavirus infections.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka, Hank Sanders and Michael Levenson
item.media:description: Hantavirus most often spreads from dried rodent droppings.

item.title: The Widow of the Firefighter Slain by a Would-Be Assassin Speaks Out
item.description: The conspiracy theories around the Butler, Pa., assassination attempt hurt Helen Comperatore, but in this age of distrust, partisanship and social media, she nurtures a conspiracy theory of her own.
item.dc:creator: Kate Zernike
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times

item.title: Is Loving Food the Secret to Eating Less of It?
item.description: The case for ditching self-denial and embracing enjoyment.
item.dc:creator: Pete Wells

item.title: How Yuppies Changed America
item.description: The young urban professionals of the 1980s changed our cities, our politics and our meritocratic work lives.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Gottlieb
item.category: Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom LLP

item.title: What on Earth Is Happening in California? Your Guide to a Very Weird Election.
item.description: Why is the country’s most populous state stuck with such a bad choice for governor?
item.dc:creator: Michelle Cottle

item.title: After the Hong Kong Fire Killed 3 Generations of His Family, A Son Searches for Justice
item.description: Lee Chun Ho lost four members of his household in last November’s apartment-tower blaze. He’s now telling an investigative committee what contractors ignored.
item.dc:creator: Anjali Tsui
item.media:credit: Billy H.C. Kwok for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lee Chun Ho (right), his father, and Mr. Lee’s sister-in-law outside Wang Fuk Court in April. Mr. Lee’s mother had been in a residents’ group that helped voice concerns about the safety of renovation works later linked to the fire.

item.title: The ‘Gates to Hell’ Are Dimming. That May Not Be a Good Thing.
item.description: A flaming crater of gas has burned in Turkmenistan for decades. Satellite imagery suggests that the fires are decreasing, but the environmental implications are unclear.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.media:description: The Darvaza gas crater, known as the “Gates to Hell,” in Turkmenistan’s Karakum Desert in 2020.

item.title: GameStop Proposes Acquiring eBay for $55 Billion
item.description: Analysts question the feasibility of this long-shot bid by the video game retailer to acquire the much larger online marketplace.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Chona Kasinger for The New York Times
item.media:description: GameStop has proposed that Ryan Cohen would serve as chief executive of a combined company.

item.title: A Strong El Niño May Be Coming. Global Warming Is Changing Its Effects.
item.description: As the planet warms, past episodes of the natural weather phenomenon may no longer be a reliable guide of how the next one plays out.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Harry Stevens
